; CHAPTER XV. ; Davia bringeth the ark. from Obed-edoin with great joy. : ND David male him houses in the city of David, and prepared aplace for the ark of God, and pitthed for it.a tent. ~
Perhaps this psalm was composed on the same occasion as the 24th and 47th, when David brought up the ark of God to that tabernacle, which he had pitched for it in Zion, 2 Sam. 6.
Probably this psalm was penned for use of the Hebrews, when David brought up the ark of God to Jerusalem, or when Solomon brought it into the temple, 2 Sam. 6, 1 Kings 8.
Whether this psalm was penned when David brought up the ark of God to Mount Zion, 2Sam. 6, or relates to the Chaldean captivity, is uncertain.
Whether this psalm was penned by David when he brought up the ark of God from Kirjath-jearim, 2Sam. 6, or for Solomon's dedication of the temple, 1Kings 8, is uncertain.

David . gathered: .all' Israel together, from Shihor of Egypt even unto the entering of Hemath, to bring the ark of God from Kirjathjearim.
; CHAPTER XV. ; Davia bringeth the ark. from Obed-edoin with great joy. : ND David male him houses in the city of David, and prepared aplace for the ark of God, and pitthed for it.a tent. ~
Perhaps this psalm was composed on the same occasion as the 24th and 47th, when David brought up the ark of God to that tabernacle, which he had pitched for it in Zion, 2 Sam. 6.
Pitched his tent toward Sodom means that he had turned his life and heart that direction.

The word tent written in reference to Jacob also carries over to Laban.42The object following pitched with reference to Laban is missing, hence I.E.’s comment. That is, now Jacob had pitched his tent in the mountain, and Laban pitched his tent with his brethren in the mountain of Gilead.
; CHAPTER XV. ; Davia bringeth the ark. from Obed-edoin with great joy. : ND David male him houses in the city of David, and prepared aplace for the ark of God, and pitthed for it.a tent. ~
Pitched his tent toward Sodom means that he had turned his life and heart that direction.

The word tent written in reference to Jacob also carries over to Laban.42The object following pitched with reference to Laban is missing, hence I.E.’s comment. That is, now Jacob had pitched his tent in the mountain, and Laban pitched his tent with his brethren in the mountain of Gilead.
Probably this psalm was penned for use of the Hebrews, when David brought up the ark of God to Jerusalem, or when Solomon brought it into the temple, 2 Sam. 6, 1 Kings 8.

Jerusalem in:.mount.Meriah, where. the Lord appeared., unto: David -his father, in the place that David had prepared in the, shreshing-taer of. Ornan. the Jebusite.
92 And Bezaleel the son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, made all that the Lord comjnanded Moses.
92 And Bezaleel the son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, made all that the Lord comjnanded Moses.

matches.lb: Quoting Commentary: Ibn Ezra on Exodus 31:2:2
matches.x: [I HAVE CALLED BY NAME BEZALEL.] Bezalel is the one being called.8The word karati (I have called) is to be connected to bezalel ben uri (Bezalel the son of Uri) rather than to the word be-shem (by name). Thus karati be-shem bezalel ben uri (I have called by name Bezalel the son of Uri) is to be rendered: I have called Bezalel the son of Uri by name. I.E. comments thus because karati be-shem bezalel ben uri could conceivably be translated: I have called upon the name of Bezalel the son of Uri. Co
92 And Bezaleel the son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, made all that the Lord comjnanded Moses.

matches.lb: Quoting Commentary: Ibn Ezra on Exodus 31:2:2
matches.x: [I HAVE CALLED BY NAME BEZALEL.] Bezalel is the one being called.8The word karati (I have called) is to be connected to bezalel ben uri (Bezalel the son of Uri) rather than to the word be-shem (by name). Thus karati be-shem bezalel ben uri (I have called by name Bezalel the son of Uri) is to be rendered: I have called Bezalel the son of Uri by name. I.E. comments thus because karati be-shem bezalel ben uri could conceivably be translated: I have called upon the name of Bezalel the son of Uri. Co
92 And Bezaleel the son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, made all that the Lord comjnanded Moses.

5 And the Lord God hearkened unto Solomon, and appeared unto him in a dream by night, and said, Ask what I shall give thee.

5 And the Lord God hearkened unto Solomon, and appeared unto him in a dream by night, and said, Ask what I shall give thee.

Ask of me and I will give you the nations. If the nations are already your inheritance, as it is said (Psalm 72:8), "May he rule from sea to sea, and from the River to the ends of the earth." And if the ends of the earth are already your possession, as it is said (ibid. 2:8), "From the rivers of Ethiopia to the ends of the earth," then what did God mean when He said to three people, "Ask of Me," namely, Solomon, Ahaz, and the Messiah? Solomon, as it is said (1 Kings 3:5), "Ask what I shall give

9 Give therefore thy servant an understanding heart to judge thy people, that I may discern between good people and bad; for who is able to judge this thy so great a people?
9 Give therefore thy servant an understanding heart to judge thy people, that I may discern between good people and bad; for who is able to judge this thy so great a people?

11. And God said unto him, Because thou hast asked this thing, and hast not asked for. thyself long life; neither hast. asked riches for thyself, nor hast asked the life of thine enemies; but hast asked. for thyself. understanding to discern judgment ;

The story of King Solomon tells us that we can ask the Lord for understanding. When Solomon was in Gibeon, the Lord appeared to him in a dream and said: "Ask what I shall give thee" (1 Kings 3:5). Solomon, feeling overwhelmed and underprepared for his new responsibilities as king, told the Lord: ". . . I am but a little child: I know not how to go out or come in" (vs. 7). Therefore, he asked the Lord for "an understanding heart to judge" the people. We read in 1 Kings, Chapter 3, that the Lord…

28 And Solomon had horses brought out of Egypt, and linen yarn; the king’s merchants received the linen yarn at a price.

In the King James Version, 1 Kings 10:28 reads: "And Solomon had horses brought out of Egypt, and linen yarn: the king's merchants received the linen yarn at a price." The New American Bible translates this passage: "Solomon's horses were imported from Cilicia, where the king's agents purchased them." The Interpreter's Bible translates it: "And Solomon's import of horses was from Egypt and Ku'e, and the king's traders received them from Ku'e at a price." The new LDS edition of the King James…

CHARIOT. A wooden chariot pulled by four horses,69Some interpret mirkevet (chariot) to mean a horse or mule. Cf. Rashi. as is evidenced by the chariot of Solomon.70We read in I Kings 10:29, And a chariot (merkavah) came up and went out of Egypt for six hundred shekels of silver, and a horse for a hundred and fifty. Now four times 150 gives 600. Hence each chariot had four horses.

_16 And Solomon had horses brought out of Egypt, and-linen yarn; the king’s merchants received the linen yaru at a price. “17 And they fetched "ps and brought forth out of Egypt a chariot for six hundred shekels: of silver, and a horse for a hundred and fifty; and so brought they out horses for all the kings of the Hittites, and for the kings of Syria, by their means.
